mnemonics.title: BFS vs DFS Properties
mnemonics.content:
BFS: Complete, Optimal (unit cost), Queue data structure, Shallowest first
DFS: Incomplete, Not optimal, Stack data structure, Deepest first

Remember: BFS uses Queue (FIFO) - Goes Wide
DFS uses Stack (LIFO) - Goes Deep

mnemonics.title: A* Evaluation Function
mnemonics.content:
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)

g = Cost from start to n (actual)
h = Estimated cost from n to goal (heuristic)
f = Total estimated solution cost

Remember: g = ground (actual path cost), h = heaven (heuristic estimate)

mnemonics.title: Search Algorithm Selection
mnemonics.content:
Want completeness? Use BFS or IDDFS
Want optimal with varying costs? Use Uniform-cost
Want fastest but not optimal? Use Greedy
Want optimal and fast? Use A* with good heuristic
Memory limited? Use IDDFS or Iterative Deepening A*

acronyms.usage: Informed search algorithm using f = g + h for optimal solution
Explores all nodes at current depth before going deeper, guarantees optimal solution for unit costs
Explores as deep as possible before backtracking, uses less memory but incomplete
Combines BFS completeness with DFS memory efficiency by progressively increasing depth limit
Problem with variables and constraints, like 8-queens, crossword puzzles
Mnemonic for search: T-Test goal, R-Retrieve, E-Expand, E-Evaluate, E-Enqueue

visualTips.visualization: Draw tree from root (initial state) spreading downward. BFS expands level by level (wide), DFS follows one branch deep before backtracking. Mark visited nodes to avoid cycles.

Think of f(n) as estimated total distance. g(n) is distance traveled, h(n) is estimated remaining distance. A* always picks node with smallest f(n), like choosing path with shortest estimated total distance.
Imagine you want to reach destination in a city. Straight-line distance (as-the-crow-flies) is admissible heuristic - actual road distance can only be equal or longer.

Think of state space as a maze. Search algorithms are different ways to explore: BFS checks all nearby corridors before going deeper, DFS picks one path until blocked, A* uses a guide (heuristic) to head toward exit.

analogies.analogy: Flooding water - spreads uniformly in all directions from start, reaching all points at distance 1 before any at distance 2.
Exploring a cave with only one path visible at a time - go as far as possible, when blocked, backtrack to last junction.
Using GPS navigation - considers both distance traveled (g) and estimated remaining distance (h) to find optimal route.
Asking local for directions - gives estimate of distance/time to goal, guides search decision.
Climbing a hill in fog - only see nearby ground, move upward from current position, may get stuck in local maximum.
Asking someone who underestimates - always says distance is same or shorter than reality, never promises shorter than possible.
